IVERSON, YOAKUM, PAPIANO & HATCH
LAWYERS
ONE WILSHIRE BUILDING
624 SOUTH GRAND AVENUE
27TH FLOOR
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017-3328
(213) 624-7444

TELECOPIER:
(213) 629-4563

PAUL E. IVERSON
(1907-1975)

R. NOEL HATCH
NEIL PAPIANO
DENNIS A. PAGE
HERBERT J. WACHTELL
ARNOLD D. LARSON
DEBORAH M. NESSET
RICHARD E. BENNETT
ELSA H. JONES
DOUGLAS C. PLEASE
MARGARET E. CARSON
MELANIE G. GOVER
ANDREW K. DOTY
BARBARA L. BERKOWITZ

FRANK B. YOAKUM, JR.
OF COUNSEL

FILE NUMBER

June 1, 1990

Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
428 J Street, Suite 600
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Commissioners:

     The purpose of this letter is to solicit your opinion on two matters on which there is some confusion after reading various statutes and the regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission.

     From time to time, one or another of my various clients, or on some occasions several of my clients, ask me to make a contribution to a political candidate or to support a proposition or other ballot measure with a contribution. On most of those occasions, because of client relationships, I do so. In addition, from time to time, I request clients to make similar contributions in response to requests made to me by a political candidate or one of his supporters or even by another client. In all of these instances the actual source of the cash is clearly shown by the name on the check. In each instance the contribution is appropriately reported by the donor and, I assume, by the donee. However, after reading the statutes and interpretive regulations I still have the following questions:

     1. When I request a contribution from one or more of my clients and I receive a check from them evidencing their contribution, if I transmit the check to the candidate or the candidate's supporters, either at a dinner or by mail, am I an "intermediary" under the terms of the reporting statutes and if so what do I do to comply with the regulations to appropriately make a report as an "intermediary"?

     2. When a client solicits support from me and I make a personal contribution evidenced by a check on which my name appears and which is appropriately reported in my name, is the person requesting the contribution an "intermediary" or does a person become an "intermediary" if I give the check to someone to deliver to the candidate or to the candidate's representative and I attend the political function or dinner for which the request

IVERSON, YOAKUM, PAPIANO & HATCH

Fair Political Practices Commission
June 4, 1990
Page 2

is made?  Is the answer different if a friend or someone in my
firm uses the tickets for which I have paid?

     It is my interpretation that the answer to all of these
questions is, "no" and that so long as the name of the person
making the contribution appears on the check and that person
reports the contribution as required along with, of course, the
appropriate report of the candidate, no further reporting is
necessary.  I should appreciate it, however, if you will confirm
my understanding in order that there can be no mistakes or future
difficulties.

     Thank you very much for your consideration of this matter
and for your response.

                                        Yours very truly,


                                        Neil Papiano
                                        of IVERSON, YOAKUM, PAPIANO & HATCH
